reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In libavcodec/aacenc.c:652 (pre-fix), s->lambda is clamped only from above via FFMIN(s->lambda, 65536.f), with no lower bound. After repeated multiplicative updates (s->lambda *= ratio at lines 640, 651), lambda can underflow to 0.0f, causing floating-point division-by-zero downstream.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: s->lambda is initialized at aacenc.c:225 and multiplicatively scaled in the rate-control loop (lines 640, 651). The one-sided clamp FFMIN (line 652) permits lambda to reach 0.0f. Lambda 0.0f is then passed to aaccoder.c functions: search_for_quantizers_faac computes 'distfact = 147.84 / lambda' (line 843), and encode_window_bands_info computes 'lambda / band->threshold' (line 353). Division by zero produces NaN/Inf, corrupting rate-distortion calculations and causing encoding crashes (denial of service).
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Replace FFMIN(s->lambda * ratio, 65536.f) with av_clipf(s->lambda * ratio, FLT_MIN, 65536.f), adding #include <float.h> for FLT_MIN. This provides a two-sided clamp: FLT_MIN (~1.175e-38) ensures lambda never reaches zero, eliminating the division-by-zero. All 7 commits apply the identical logical change; a7a7f32c8ad0 is the original fix, the other 6 are cherry-picks.

necessary_conditions: s->lambda must reach 0.0f through repeated multiplicative scaling (lambda *= ratio) combined with only an upper-bound clamp (FFMIN).
The lambda value must flow into a division operation in aaccoder.c (search_for_quantizers_faac: 147.84 / lambda, or encode_window_bands_info: lambda / band->threshold).
The encoder must be processing a frame with bitrate/samplerate configuration that causes ratio to be small enough for the multiplicative chain to drive lambda to zero.

s->lambda *= ratio;  (aacenc.c:651)
s->lambda = FFMIN(s->lambda, 65536.f);  (aacenc.c:652) — one-sided upper clamp, no lower bound
s->coder->search_for_quantizers(avctx, s, &cpe->ch[ch], s->lambda);  (aacenc.c:593) — lambda=0 flows into search_for_quantizers_faac
float distfact = 147.84 / lambda;  (aaccoder.c:843) — DIVISION BY ZERO when lambda=0
missing_guards: No lower-bound guard on s->lambda before or after the multiplicative updates in the rate-control loop.
No guard checking lambda==0 before division in aaccoder.c search_for_quantizers_faac or encode_window_bands_info.

fix_guards: av_clipf(s->lambda * ratio, FLT_MIN, 65536.f) — two-sided clamp with FLT_MIN as the non-zero lower bound.
fix_evidence.semantic_change: Changed from a one-sided upper clamp to a two-sided clip. FFMIN only prevents lambda from exceeding 65536.f but allows lambda to reach 0. av_clipf with FLT_MIN ensures lambda never drops below the minimum positive float, preventing division-by-zero in downstream rate-distortion calculations.

preconditions: FFMIN (one-sided clamp) must be the only protection on lambda after rate-control updates.
The downstream division operations (aaccoder.c) must receive the non-zero-clamped lambda as their divisor.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: Memory leak (CWE-401) in ff_frame_thread_encoder_init: avcodec_alloc_context3() allocations for per-thread AVCodecContext instances were never freed on error paths. The variable was block-scoped inside a for-loop making it unreachable for cleanup at the fail: label. The fail: label had no avcodec_close()/av_freep() calls. Additionally, the struct copy *thread_avctx = *avctx propagated shared internal and hw_frames_ctx pointers that were only NULL'd after av_opt_copy (or not at all for hw_frames_ctx), creating double-free risk on partial failure.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: In ff_frame_thread_encoder_init(), the loop for(i=0; i<avctx->thread_count; i++) declares a block-scoped AVCodecContext *thread_avctx = avcodec_alloc_context3(avctx->codec). After allocation, *thread_avctx = *avctx copies all pointer fields from parent avctx including internal and hw_frames_ctx. If av_opt_copy(), avcodec_open2(), or pthread_create() fails on the current or a subsequent iteration, goto fail is taken. The fail: label sets avctx->thread_count = i and calls ff_frame_thread_encoder_free(avctx) which only cleans worker threads that were fully initialized (pthread_create succeeded). The current iteration's thread_avctx heap block is never freed. Additionally, internal/hw_frames_ctx pointers pointing to parent avctx's shared resources were not NULL'd before the goto fail, so if ff_frame_thread_encoder_free or any later code path attempts to free these shared pointers (via avcodec_close on surviving thread_avctx copies), a double-free occurs. The hw_frames_ctx was never set to NULL at any point in the vulnerable code.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Three structural changes in libavcodec/frame_thread_encoder.c: (1) Moved AVCodecContext *thread_avctx = NULL to function scope (outside the loop), changing the loop allocation from block-scoped declaration to assignment thread_avctx = avcodec_alloc_context3(avctx->codec). This makes thread_avctx accessible at the fail: label. (2) Moved thread_avctx->priv_data = tmpv and thread_avctx->internal = NULL to immediately after *thread_avctx = *avctx (before av_opt_copy), eliminating the window where av_opt_copy failure would skip these NULL-assignments. Added thread_avctx->hw_frames_ctx = NULL for the same reason. (3) Added cleanup at fail:: avcodec_close(thread_avctx); av_freep(&thread_avctx); which properly frees the in-flight thread_avctx allocation on any error path. The header change in libavcodec/frame_thread_encoder.h is a doxygen comment (cosmetic, not security-relevant).

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In RestoreMSCWarning() in coders/pdf.c, GetPixelIndex() returns a Quantum (unsigned short on default Q16 builds or wider), but its value is directly cast to (unsigned char) at 4 call sites. When a pixel index exceeds 255, high-order bits are silently truncated (CWE-190 integer wraparound). No intermediate wider-type cast or bounds check exists. The fix inserts (ssize_t) to provide a well-defined conversion pathway.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: GetPixelIndex() returns Quantum (typedef unsigned short on default Q16 builds, 16-bit). At 4 locations inside pixel-column loops in RestoreMSCWarning(), the return value is directly cast to (unsigned char): three via *q++=(unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(...) lines 1992/2509 and one via Ascii85Encode(image,(unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(...)) lines 2034/2546. No intermediate type wider than unsigned char precedes the truncation. When a crafted PDF produces a pixel index > 255, the direct (unsigned char) cast discards excess bits, corrupting output pixel data. Impact is limited to data corruption/availability (CVSS 3.3 LOW). The vulnerability is only triggerable on Q16+ Quantum depth builds (default).
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Each vulnerable call site is patched by inserting ((ssize_t) ...) between GetPixelIndex() and the outer (unsigned char) cast, transforming the pattern from (unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(...) to (unsigned char) ((ssize_t) GetPixelIndex(...)). ssize_t is wide enough (64-bit on LP64) to hold any Quantum value. The fix spans 2 linear commits in commit-date order: 872ffe6d0131 (first, 19:38:22, fixes line 2546), then 9dd1c7e1f8f6 (19:43:23, parent=872ffe6d0131, fixes lines 1992/2034/2509). Both reference github issue #1753.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In DrawPrimitive (MagickCore/draw.c), SetImageInfo is called with frames=0 when resolving an SVG image-href path. With frames=0, SetImageInfo opens the target file and reads its magic bytes to auto-detect the image format, which triggers re-entrant decoding. A crafted SVG referencing a path that re-enters the SVG parser causes infinite recursion / resource exhaustion (DoS).
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: When DrawPrimitive processes an SVG <image> element whose href is neither a 'data:' URI nor empty, it copies the text value to clone_info->filename and calls SetImageInfo(clone_info, 0, exception) at draw.c:5538. The frames=0 argument causes SetImageInfo to: (1) resolve the filename via CanonicalPath (which strips [scene] subimage specs), then (2) enter the 'if (frames == 0)' block that opens the file with OpenBlob, reads magic bytes via GetMagicPatternExtent, and determines the image format from content rather than extension. For a crafted SVG that points to another SVG or to a path that triggers SVG re-decoding, this file-open + format-detection step causes the decoder to call back into the SVG parser, which again calls DrawPrimitive for embedded image tags, creating a recursive decode loop. The recursion exhausts the call stack or system resources, resulting in denial of service.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: The fix changes the second argument of SetImageInfo from 0 to 1 (frames=1). With frames != 0, SetImageInfo uses SubcanonicalPath (skipping [scene] spec parsing) and — critically — skips the entire 'if (frames == 0)' magic-byte detection block. This means SetImageInfo no longer opens the file to sniff its content, preventing the re-entrant decoder trigger. The filename is still copied back and subsequently checked by IsPathAccessible/ReadImage, but the re-entrant decode via magic-byte detection is eliminated.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: Two distinct flaws in ImageMagick module policy enforcement: (1) RegisterStaticModules() completely lacked any IsRightsAuthorized call, so statically-linked modules were registered unconditionally regardless of user module policy; (2) RegisterStaticModule() and OpenModule() used ReadPolicyRights (0x01) instead of ReadPolicyRights|WritePolicyRights (0x03), so IsRightsAuthorized never validated WritePolicyRights authorization — a policy granting read-only would incorrectly allow module registration despite the module subsequently performing write operations.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: When ImageMagick initializes, RegisterStaticModules() is called to register all statically-compiled module handlers. This function iterated the MagickModules[] array calling each register_module() function pointer with zero policy enforcement. Separately, when individual modules were loaded via RegisterStaticModule() or OpenModule(), the IsRightsAuthorized rights mask was ReadPolicyRights (0x01). Since IsRightsAuthorized only checks rights bits set in the caller-provided mask, WritePolicyRights (0x02) authorization was never validated. A policy granting read-only (rights="read") would pass despite write rights not being granted. Combined effect: modules excluded by the user's <policy domain="module" rights="none" pattern="PS" /> in policy.xml could still be loaded and used.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Commit 1 (35893e7cad78) added the missing IsRightsAuthorized call to RegisterStaticModules(). Commit 2 (01faddbe2711) corrected the rights mask in all three functions from AllPolicyRights (0xff) or ReadPolicyRights (0x01) to ReadPolicyRights|WritePolicyRights (0x03), which is the semantically correct mask for module operations.

The restricted coder is compiled as a static module in ImageMagick's MagickModules[] array

OR RegisterStaticModule()/OpenModule() is called for a module with a policy that grants read but restricts write

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In curl_wssl_init_ctx(), four wolfSSL error paths (cipher set failure, curves set failure, keylog callback not built, CA cert load failure) execute goto out without assigning result. If result is CURLE_OK (e.g. because ctx_setup returned success), the out: cleanup if(result && ctx->ssl_ctx) evaluates false since CURLE_OK is 0, so the SSL context is not freed and CURLE_OK is returned. The caller treats TLS init as successful and proceeds without proper certificate verification.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: The function initializes result = CURLE_FAILED_INIT, then calls ctx_setup (if non-NULL) which can set result = CURLE_OK. Four subsequent wolfSSL operations each have error paths that jump to out without updating result. The out: label checks if(result && ctx->ssl_ctx). When result is CURLE_OK (0), this guard is false: the SSL_CTX is leaked and CURLE_OK is returned. The QUIC connection continues without configured ciphers/curves/CA certificates, bypassing certificate verification.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Adds 4 error code assignments before each goto out: result = CURLE_BAD_FUNCTION_ARGUMENT for cipher and curves failures, result = CURLE_NOT_BUILT_IN for keylog callback not available, result = CURLE_SSL_CACERT for CA cert load failure. This ensures the function always returns the correct specific error code instead of leaking CURLE_OK.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In utf8asn1str(), the local variable `buf` is declared as `char buf[4]` (stack-allocated). On the error path when the decoded Unicode codepoint exceeds the valid UTF-8 range (wc >= 0x00200000), the code calls `free(buf)` on this stack buffer. Freeing a non-heap pointer is undefined behavior: on some malloc implementations it causes immediate abort, on others it corrupts heap metadata by treating the stack address as a heap chunk header, leading to memory corruption and potential denial of service (crash) or more severe consequences.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: When libcurl's ASN.1 parser processes an ASN.1 UTF-8 string via utf8asn1str(), multi-byte character types (BMP_STRING with size=2 or UNIVERSAL_STRING with size=4) trigger a character decoding loop. If the decoded codepoint is >= 0x00200000 (outside the valid Unicode range representable in UTF-8), the code enters an error path that calls `free(buf)` where `buf` is a 4-byte stack-allocated array. This is a use-after-free variant: the pointer was never returned by malloc/calloc/realloc, so free() operates on invalid memory. An attacker who controls the ASN.1 certificate content can trigger this path, causing heap metadata corruption or process termination.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Remove the single line `free(buf);` from the error path. Since `buf` is declared as a local stack variable (`char buf[4]` inside the while loop body), it requires no deallocation. The error return (`return CURLE_WEIRD_SERVER_REPLY;`) is sufficient — the stack frame will be cleaned up automatically on function return. No other changes are needed.

      char buf[4]; /* decode buffer */

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: curl's metalink download feature has a design-level integrity failure: when `metalink_check_hash()` detects a digest mismatch after a completed download, the tampered content is neither deleted nor quarantined, and the CURLE_OK result code is not overridden. While `metalink_next_res = 1` causes a retry from the next mirror URL, the bad file persists on disk until/unless an overwrite succeeds, and no error is propagated to the caller. The fix commit explicitly acknowledges this as an unfixable design problem at the metalink library API/RFC level, resulting in total removal of the feature.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: In `post_per_transfer()` at `src/tool_operate.c:431-436` (old code), after a successful HTTP download, `metalink_check_hash()` compares the downloaded file's digest against the expected hash from the metalink XML. On mismatch, `rv=0` and `per->metalink_next_res=1` to retry the next mirror URL. However: (1) the tampered file at `per->outfile` is never deleted or truncated before the retry; (2) the function variable `result` remains `CURLE_OK`, so no error is signaled up the call stack; (3) the retry writes to the same `outs->filename`, so only a fully-successful second download overwrites the bad content — if the last resource also fails, the tampered file is retained silently. Additionally, the TODO comment at line 608-609 (`"TODO We want to try next resource when download was not successful. How to know that?"`) confirms the developer recognized the retry-detection gap was unresolved. The `post_per_transfer` metalink branch at line 603 (`else if(per->metalink)`) handles HTTP status failures (non-200/206) and transport errors by setting `metalink_next_res=1`, but never cleans up the output file.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Complete removal of the entire metalink feature: all code in `src/tool_metalink.c`, `src/tool_metalink.h`, the `#ifdef USE_METALINK` blocks in `src/tool_operate.c`, `src/tool_getparam.c`, `src/tool_help.c`, `src/tool_cfgable.h`, `src/tool_operate.h`, `src/tool_operhlp.c`, `src/tool_main.c`, `src/tool_sdecls.h`, and all test fixtures (`tests/data/test2005`–`test2022`). The commit message lists 6 reasons including "several security problems" inherent to the metalink design/library API. The fix replaces the `--metalink` option handler in `getparameter()` with a hard error (`return PARAM_BAD_USE`) and removes the `featcomp`/`tool_version_info` metalink version string integration. The `metadata` fields (`per->metalink`, `per->mlfile`, `per->mlres`, `outs->metalink_parser`) and related config struct members are all removed.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In remoteip_modify_request, the trust anchor for verifying proxy IPs was unconditionally set to c->client_addr (the TCP connection address). On internal subrequests/redirects (e.g., ErrorDocument triggered by mod_rewrite or 4xx responses), c->client_addr remains the upstream proxy's IP from the original connection, not the client IP. Since the proxy IP is trusted, mod_remoteip re-processes X-Forwarded-For headers on the subrequest, allowing an attacker-controlled XFF value to overwrite the previously verified r->useragent_addr.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: 1) A request arrives through a trusted proxy; mod_remoteip processes XFF headers, correctly sets r->useragent_addr to the client IP. 2) An internal subrequest/redirect is generated (ErrorDocument, mod_rewrite [PT]). 3) mod_remoteip's remoteip_modify_request runs again on the subrequest. 4) c->client_addr is unchanged — still the proxy's trusted IP. 5) temp_sa = c->client_addr sets the trust anchor to the proxy IP. 6) The trust check passes (proxy is trusted), so the XFF processing loop re-enters. 7) The attacker-controlled XFF values are processed again, overwriting r->useragent_addr with a spoofed IP. 8) Downstream handlers (logging, PHP $_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR']) receive the spoofed IP. This is CWE-345: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity — the code fails to verify that re-processing XFF on a subrequest is safe.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Changed temp_sa = c->client_addr to temp_sa = r->useragent_addr ? r->useragent_addr : c->client_addr. On the first invocation (main request), r->useragent_addr is NULL, so c->client_addr is used (preserving original trust behavior). On subrequests, r->useragent_addr is already set from the first pass and becomes the trust anchor. Since the real client IP is not a trusted proxy, the loop immediately breaks, preventing re-processing and preserving the correct r->useragent_addr.

source_refs.snippet: mod_remoteip: Use r->useragent_addr as the root trusted address for verifying. This fixes issue resulting in setting of bad useragent_ip when internal redirection has been generated as response to the request (typically as result of "ErrorDocument 40x").

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: CVE-2020-9490 is a DoS vulnerability in Apache HTTP Server's mod_http2 caused by two independent root-cause families: (1) unconditional header counter increment before validation and missing merged-field-size enforcement in h2_stream.c/h2_request.c/h2_util.c, and (2) unsigned integer underflow in the push diary's move_to_last() when diary->entries->nelts is 0, leading to out-of-bounds memory access.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: Root Cause 1 (header counting bypass - CWE-444): In h2_stream_add_header() (h2_stream.c), ++stream->request_headers_added was executed unconditionally at function entry, before any header validation or state check. Repeated headers with same name counted separately against LimitRequestFields even though they merge internally. Field size check was in else-if chain that skipped for pseudo-headers and did not account for existing merged value lengths. h2_req_add_header() and add_trailer() did not check if merging with existing header would exceed limit_req_fieldsize, allowing unbounded allocation. set_h1_header() callback returned 0 on failure, potentially aborting header table processing. Root Cause 2 (push diary integer underflow - CWE-190): In move_to_last() (h2_push.c), 'apr_size_t lastidx = diary->entries->nelts-1' underflows to SIZE_MAX when nelts==0. No guard for empty diary before memmove() with massive copy size and return of out-of-bounds pointer &entries[lastidx]. Cache-Digest header processing could initialize empty diary, triggering this path on subsequent PUSH.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Commit 971fc8f5b5d6: Restructured h2_stream_add_header() with goto cleanup pattern. Moved ++stream->request_headers_added from unconditional top-of-function to conditional 'if (was_added)' increments. Moved field-size checking into h2_req_add_header()/add_trailer() where merged size is compared against max_field_len before allocation. Added max_field_len and pwas_added parameters throughout call chain. Changed set_h1_header() to always return 1. Commit a61223e9cb90: Changed move_to_last() return type from pointer to void, added 'if (diary->entries->nelts <= 0) return;' guard, changed lastidx from unsigned apr_size_t to signed int. Refactored h2_push_diary_append() from if/else to while(>=N){remove_first()}+unconditional push. Removed entire Cache-Digest client-supplied digest feature.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In Apache HTTP Server mod_http2, slave/secondary connections that process HTTP/2 streams ran with their own memory pools and could execute in separate threads from the master connection. When trace/debug logging (APLOG_TRACE1-3) was enabled, multiple logging call sites used the master connection pointer (m->c) instead of the slave/secondary connection pointer for log operations that actually belonged to the stream-processing thread. This caused concurrent access to the master connection's memory pool from multiple threads, leading to pool corruption, use-after-free, or crashes. Additionally, in h2_stream_add_header, the request_headers_added counter was incremented unconditionally before validation, allowing the counter to desynchronize from actual header storage.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism:
The mod_http2 module created 'slave' connections (conn_rec) with independent memory pools and allocators for each HTTP/2 stream, allowing stream processing to occur in separate threads from the master HTTP/2 connection. The module's logging infrastructure used the master connection's log context (m->c) at multiple call sites that executed in the context of slave/stream threads. Specifically:

(1) In out_open(), h2_beam_log() and ap_log_cerror() used m->c instead of stream->task->c (the correct stream-specific connection). Since these log calls execute at APLOG_TRACE2/TRACE1 levels, they only activate when debug logging is enabled (LogLevel above 'info'), making this a conditional race.

(2) In stream_destroy_iter(), h2_beam_log() calls for stream input/output beams used m->c.

(3) In task_done(), multiple ap_log_cerror() calls used m->c instead of task->c.

(4) In out_close(), ap_log_cerror() and h2_beam_log() used m->c instead of task->c.

(5) In h2_slave_create() / h2_secondary_create(), the slave pool tag was 'h2_slave_conn'.

(6) In h2_stream_add_header (commit 971fc8f5b5d6), ++stream->request_headers_added was executed unconditionally before error checking, meaning even when a header was rejected (too long, invalid state), the counter still incremented. After enough rejected headers, valid headers would also be rejected, and the hard limit check (limit_req_fields + 100) could prevent any further request processing.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism:
The fix uses two complementary strategies:

Commit 63a0a87efa09 (structural fix):
- Renamed all 'slave' terminology to 'secondary' to semantically clarify the connection role separation.
- All static functions were renamed with thread-context prefix conventions: m_* (master thread only), s_* (secondary/stream thread only), t_* (task thread only), mst_* (any thread, must be thread-safe).
- Critical logging call sites were corrected to use the correct connection pointer: m->c was replaced with task->c, stream->task->c, secondary, or the appropriate connection-specific pointer.
- Added proper null-pointer guards (if (secondary) { ... }) before accessing secondary connection fields.
- Renamed purge_streams to m_purge_streams, check_data_for to mst_check_data_for, etc. to prevent cross-thread confusion.
- Changed h2_mplx_stream_get (which took an int id) to h2_mplx_t_stream_get (which takes a task pointer) to avoid potential ID reuse issues.

Commit 971fc8f5b5d6 (header counting fix):
- Changed ++stream->request_headers_added (unconditional increment) to use a was_added flag returned from h2_request_add_header()/add_trailer(). Only when the header was actually stored does the counter increment.
- Added early-exit goto cleanup paths for error conditions, preventing stale counter increments.
- Added explicit status checks: if (APR_EINVAL == status) { goto cleanup; }
- Added an early check: if limit_req_fields > 0 and already over limit, count the attempt but skip storage.
- Hard limit protection: if stream->request_headers_added > session->s->limit_req_fields + 100, reject further headers unconditionally.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: Unix domain socket garbage collector (unix_gc) frees sk_buff entries carrying io_uring registered files via __skb_queue_purge, but io_uring holds independent references to those files through its registered file table. After the skb's destructor (unix_destruct_scm) puts the file references, io_uring accesses already-freed file structures, causing a Use-After-Free.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: When io_uring registers files via __io_scm_file_account, the files are sent through Unix SCM_RIGHTS. The created sk_buff is linked into the Unix socket's memory accounting via skb->sk assignment, unix_destruct_scm destructor, and refcount_add on sk_wmem_alloc. When unix_gc() performs cycle detection on unreachable SCM file references, it collects these sk_buff entries into a hitlist and purges them via __skb_queue_purge(&hitlist). This calls unix_destruct_scm which puts the file references, reducing their refcount to zero and freeing them. However, io_uring's registered file table still holds references to these files and accesses them afterward, producing a UAF. The core problem is that unix_gc has no awareness of io_uring's separate reference domain and treats all SKBs in the hitlist as safe to free.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Add a scm_io_uring flag bit to struct sk_buff to mark SKBs carrying io_uring registered files. Set this flag in __io_scm_file_account when io_uring sends files via SCM_RIGHTS. Before unix_gc purges the hitlist, walk it with skb_queue_walk_safe and check skb->scm_io_uring: if set, unlink the SKB from the hitlist via __skb_unlink and re-queue it to the socket's receive queue via skb_queue_tail, preventing its destruction. After the purge, move io_uring GC candidates from gc_candidates back to gc_inflight_list via list_move_tail, so they survive GC and are cleaned up naturally when io_uring's io_ring_ctx_free() releases the registered files.

source_refs.snippet: skb_queue_walk_safe(&hitlist, skb, next_skb) { if (skb->scm_io_uring) { __skb_unlink(skb, &hitlist); skb_queue_tail(&skb->sk->sk_receive_queue, skb); } } ... list_for_each_entry_safe(u, next, &gc_candidates, link) list_move_tail(&u->link, &gc_inflight_list);

Instead of putting io_uring's registered files in unix_gc() we want it to be done by io_uring itself. The trick here is to consider io_uring registered files for cycle detection but not actually putting them down.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In drivers/char/pcmcia/scr24x_cs.c, scr24x_open() dereferences struct scr24x_dev from inode->i_cdev and calls kref_get() without synchronization. A concurrently executing scr24x_remove() (triggered by physically removing the PCMCIA card) calls cdev_del() and then kref_put(), which can drop dev->refcnt to zero and kfree(dev) while scr24x_open() still holds the stale dev pointer. This causes a write-after-free on the kref structure, and the freed pointer is also stored in filp->private_data for subsequent read/write/release operations.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: The race window is between container_of(inode->i_cdev) obtaining the dev pointer and kref_get(&dev->refcnt) in scr24x_open(). scr24x_remove() uses a separate kref lifecycle (init in probe, get in open, put in release/remove) that is independent from the VFS cdev kobject refcount. During the race: (1) scr24x_remove() calls cdev_del() which decrements the cdev's kobject ref — the VFS chrdev_open holds a kobject_get preventing cdev release, but the cdev is embedded in scr24x_dev; (2) scr24x_remove() sets dev->dev = NULL under dev->lock; (3) scr24x_remove() calls kref_put(&dev->refcnt, scr24x_delete) which drops the only remaining scr24x-internal reference (from probe), triggering kfree(dev); (4) scr24x_open() then writes to dev->refcnt via kref_get() on freed memory. The same dangling pointer is stored in filp->private_data, enabling subsequent read/write/release operations on freed memory.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: The fix commit 9b12f050c76f ("char: pcmcia: remove all the drivers") deletes all four PCMCIA char drivers (cm4000_cs.c, cm4040_cs.c, scr24x_cs.c, synclink_cs.c), their headers, Kconfig/Makefile entries, MAINTAINERS entry, documentation, and the UAPI header. This is a complete driver removal — the vulnerable code branch is eliminated entirely. The commit message explicitly states these drivers are 'buggy and receive only minimal care' and the removal was prompted by bug reports including this CVE.

components: SCR24x PCMCIA Smart Card Reader driver

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: smb2_get_data_area_len in fs/ksmbd/smb2misc.c had an early-return path for SMB2 requests with non-zero hdr->Status (intended as 'error requests'). This return bypassed the switch-statement that computes the data area offset/length and the subsequent validation bounds-checks. The function returned ret=0 (success) with *off=0, *len=0, causing smb2_calc_size to compute an incorrect, underestimated message size. The undersized message passed all buffer-bound checks, but the command handler (e.g. smb2_tree_connect) then read PathLength directly from the unvalidated request and passed it to smb_strndup_from_utf16, causing an out-of-bounds read.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: Attacker sends a crafted SMB2_TREE_CONNECT request with (1) hdr->Status set to a non-zero value, (2) StructureSize matching SMB2_ERROR_STRUCTURE_SIZE2_LE, and (3) a large PathLength. The early-return at lines 93-96 of the parent commit causes smb2_get_data_area_len to return success without computing or validating the data area. smb2_calc_size computes a small *len (just header + fixed params). This undersized message is accepted. smb2_tree_connect then reads req->PathLength directly from the request and passes it to smb_strndup_from_utf16, which reads PathLength bytes from the buffer, producing a KASAN slab-out-of-bounds read (CWE-125).
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Delete the 4-line early-return block (comment + 3-line if-check) entirely. After removal, all incoming SMB2 requests unconditionally enter the switch-statement that computes *off and *len from command-specific fields, then fall through to the validation checks (*off > 4096, *off+*len > MAX_STREAM_PROT_LEN). The validation now catches oversized PathLength values and returns -EINVAL, preventing the OOB read. The check was server-irrelevant; error-response shortcuts only apply to SMB clients, not servers.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions() in src/lib/openjp2/j2k.c casts p_image->x0/y0/x1/y1 (OPJ_UINT32) to OPJ_INT32 without verifying they do not exceed INT_MAX. When any coordinate exceeds INT_MAX, the cast produces a negative int32 value. These negative values propagate through component offset and dimension calculations (opj_int_ceildiv), causing final component dimensions (l_w, l_h) to wrap or become excessively large. The corrupted dimensions are stored in image components and later used in t1.c's opj_t1_clbl_decode_processor for buffer allocation and access, triggering a heap-based buffer overflow (CWE-787).
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: The loop body in opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions() directly casts p_image->x0/y0/x1/y1 from OPJ_UINT32 to OPJ_INT32 via opj_int_ceildiv((OPJ_INT32)p_image->x0, ...). No guard checks whether these uint32 values exceed INT_MAX before the cast. A crafted JPEG2000 image can specify coordinates > 2^31-1, causing signed integer truncation on cast. The resulting negative offsets in l_img_comp->x0/y0 and corrupted l_w/l_h propagate into component allocation and decoder buffers, producing out-of-bounds writes in opj_t1_clbl_decode_processor.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: An early-return guard is inserted at the top of the for-loop body, before any coordinate arithmetic. It checks each of p_image->x0, y0, x1, y1 against (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX. If any exceeds the threshold, it logs an error via opj_event_msg and returns OPJ_FALSE, rejecting the malformed image. This prevents the unsafe cast-to-int32 from ever executing on out-of-range coordinates.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In pngtoimage(), the computation of image->x1 and image->y1 double-counts the image offset: formula `(image->x0 + (width-1)*dx + 1 + image->x0)` adds x0 twice. When the `-d` option sets non-zero image_offset_x0/y0, the inflated end-coordinate propagates through the SIZ marker and tile dimension calculations, causing heap-buffer OOB access on the correctly-sized component data buffer.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: At src/bin/jp2/convertpng.c:225-228, image->x0 appears twice in the x1 computation. User-controlled params->image_offset_x0 (from -d) is the source. The inflated x1 is written to the JPEG-2000 SIZ marker (j2k.c:1982) and used for tile span calculation (j2k.c:8045-8057, tcd.c:771-789). Component data arrays are allocated as comp->w = width (the actual PNG width), so the inflated tile span causes out-of-bounds read/write.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Removed the spurious `+ image->x0` and `+ image->y0` terms from lines 226 and 228 of convertpng.c, correcting to `x1 = x0 + (width-1)*dx + 1`.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: opj_dwt_calc_explicit_stepsizes accesses opj_dwt_norms_real[orient][level] as a direct 2D array index without bounds checking on level. The array is static const [4][10]; level is computed as tccp->numresolutions - 1 - resno where tccp->numresolutions is attacker-controlled from the JPEG2000 codestream header. When numresolutions > 11, level >= 10 (for orient==0) or level >= 9 (for orient>0), resulting in an out-of-bounds read.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: An attacker supplies a JPEG2000 codestream with an excessively large number of decomposition levels (numresolutions > 11). In opj_dwt_calc_explicit_stepsizes, the loop for bandno derives level = numresolutions - 1 - resno, which can reach values >= 10 for the LL band (orient==0) or >= 9 for HL/LH/HH bands (orient>0). The direct access opj_dwt_norms_real[orient][level] reads beyond the [4][10] static array, causing a global buffer over-read (CWE-125). The read value is used as a divisor: stepsize = (1 << gain) / norm, so an out-of-range read produces undefined denominator → undefined behavior in the encoder output.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Replace the direct array access opj_dwt_norms_real[orient][level] with a call to opj_dwt_getnorm_real(level, orient), a bounds-checked wrapper that already existed in the same file. The wrapper clamps level: if orient==0 and level>=10 → level=9; if orient>0 and level>=9 → level=8. This prevents the OOB read while preserving the encoder's behavior for in-range decomposition levels.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial() and ossl_ffc_validate_private_key() in crypto/ffc/ffc_key_validate.c lack NULL pointer checks before dereferencing their parameters. In ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial, BN_copy(tmp, params->p) is called without checking whether params, pub_key, or params->p is NULL. BN_copy does not sanitize NULL inputs, so params->p == NULL causes a NULL pointer dereference and crash. In ossl_ffc_validate_private_key, BN_cmp(priv, BN_value_one()) and BN_cmp(priv, upper) are called without checking priv or upper for NULL; although BN_cmp has internal NULL handling, the functions still lack explicit input validation.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: An attacker provides a malformed DSA or DH public key via EVP_PKEY_public_check() where the FFC domain parameters have params->p == NULL, or where pub_key/priv_key is NULL. Call chain: EVP_PKEY_public_check() -> provider keymgmt_check -> ossl_dsa_check_pub_key() / DH_check_pub_key() -> ossl_ffc_validate_public_key() -> ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial(). Inside ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial, execution reaches BN_copy(tmp, params->p) on line 41 of the vulnerable code, which dereferences NULL params->p. BN_copy internally calls memcpy(NULL, ...), causing a segfault. For the private key path: ossl_dsa_check_priv_key() / ossl_dh_check_priv_key() -> ossl_ffc_validate_private_key(), where BN_cmp(priv, ...) is called on NULL priv, giving undefined behavior. The TLS implementation does not call this function directly, but applications using EVP_PKEY_public_check() on untrusted keys are vulnerable to DoS.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Three coordinated changes: (1) In ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial, a NULL guard is added at function entry: if (params == NULL || pub_key == NULL || params->p == NULL) { *ret = FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM; return 0; }. (2) In ossl_ffc_validate_private_key, a NULL guard is added: if (priv == NULL || upper == NULL) { *ret = FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM; goto err; }. (3) A new error flag constant FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM (0x40) is defined in include/internal/ffc.h to report the specific failure. Test cases in test/ffc_internal_test.c validate that passing NULL to either function returns the new error code instead of crashing.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: Fencepost (off-by-one) error: the grandloop in aesni_ocb_encrypt and aesni_ocb_decrypt uses jb (jump below) instead of jbe (jump below or equal) to test whether a full 96-byte chunk remains. $inp points past the chunk just processed; when $inp == $len exactly 96 bytes remain, but jb falls through to the short-path, skipping the last 16-byte block.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: The assembly initializes $len to the true input length minus 96 bytes and treats it as an end pointer. Each grandloop iteration processes 6x16-byte blocks then advances $inp by 96. The loop tail compares $inp to $len. With jb (signed below), equality falls through to the short handler, which expects <=5 blocks. This means when the input is a multiple of 96 bytes, the last 16-byte block is never encrypted/decrypted. The corresponding output bytes are never written (leaking uninitialized memory) and the OCB authentication tag is computed over incomplete ciphertext.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Change jb to jbe in both hunks so the grandloop iterates when $inp == $len, correctly processing the final 96-byte chunk before falling through to short.

components: AES-NI OCB assembly implementation for 32-bit x86

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: Non-recursive write lock x->lock acquired twice by the same thread: caller ossl_policy_cache_set holds it, then ossl_policy_cache_set_mapping attempts to acquire it again at bad_mapping: label when ret == -1. On Windows (CRITICAL_SECTION or SRWLOCK) this deadlocks the process.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: In ossl_policy_cache_set_mapping (crypto/x509/pcy_map.c), the bad_mapping: label contains a block (added by commit 9aa4be691f to fix a Coverity data-race warning) that does CRYPTO_THREAD_write_lock(x->lock) while the caller ossl_policy_cache_set → policy_cache_new already holds x->lock via CRYPTO_THREAD_write_lock. This creates a recursive lock acquisition on a non-recursive synchronization primitive. On Windows, EnterCriticalSection or AcquireSRWLockExclusive do not support recursive locking — the thread blocks indefinitely. The deadlock triggers when ret == -1, which happens for empty mappings, anyPolicy mapping constraints, or memory allocation failures, all reachable by a crafted X.509 certificate.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Remove the entire if-block at bad_mapping: that redundantly takes x->lock and sets x->ex_flags |= EXFLAG_INVALID_POLICY. The flag setting is already performed by the caller's bad_cache: label in policy_cache_new (crypto/x509/pcy_cache.c:168-169) which also handles the ossl_policy_cache_set_mapping return value ≤ 0. The lock is already held by the caller. This effectively reverts the bug-introducing change from commit 9aa4be691f.

source_refs.snippet: On Windows: CRYPTO_THREAD_write_lock uses EnterCriticalSection (non-recursive CS) or AcquireSRWLockExclusive, neither supports recursive locking from the same thread.

The deadlock-vulnerable block was not guarded by a platform check for recursive lock support

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: The USB xHCI controller emulation (hw/usb/hcd-xhci.c) contains two unbounded while(1) loops in xhci_ring_fetch() and xhci_ring_chain_length() that traverse a ring of Transfer Request Blocks (TRBs). When a TR_LINK type TRB is encountered, the loops follow the link pointer to a new ring location and continue iterating without counting how many links have been traversed. A privileged guest can construct a circular chain of LINK TRBs, causing the loops to never terminate and hanging the QEMU host process.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: Both xhci_ring_fetch() [parent:999-1035] and xhci_ring_chain_length() [parent:1037-1092] use while(1) loops to scan a guest-controlled TRB ring. In each iteration, the code reads a TRB from guest memory via pci_dma_read. If the TRB type is TR_LINK, the else-branch (xhci_ring_fetch) or if-branch (xhci_ring_chain_length) updates the dequeue pointer from trb->parameter and continues the loop — with no bound on the number of consecutive LINK TRBs followed. A malicious guest that creates a cycle of LINK TRBs (e.g., TRB_A.parameter -> TRB_B.parameter -> TRB_A.parameter) causes the loop to spin forever, since the only termination conditions are (1) a TRB with mismatched cycle bit, (2) a non-LINK TRB, or (3) reaching the end of a non-circular chain — none of which occur in a crafted circular LINK chain.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: The fix adds a per-invocation iteration limit on consecutive LINK TRB traversal. At line 57 (parent: N/A, added), a new macro #define TRB_LINK_LIMIT 4 is defined. In xhci_ring_fetch(), a uint32_t link_cnt = 0 counter is initialized [fix line 1005], and in the TR_LINK else-branch the guard if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return 0; } [fix lines 1032-1034] is inserted before following the link pointer. Similarly in xhci_ring_chain_length(), link_cnt = 0 is added [fix line 1052] and the guard if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return -length; } [fix lines 1068-1070] is placed in the TR_LINK branch before following the link. After TRB_LINK_LIMIT consecutive LINK TRBs, the functions return early — 0 (no TRB found) for xhci_ring_fetch, and -length (error) for xhci_ring_chain_length — breaking the infinite loop.

fix_evidence.semantic_change: Both loops now enforce a maximum number of consecutive LINK TRB traversals (TRB_LINK_LIMIT=4). Once the limit is exceeded, the functions bail out early — returning 0 from xhci_ring_fetch (treating it as end-of-ring) and -length from xhci_ring_chain_length (negative return signals error). This breaks any circular LINK TRB chain that would previously cause an infinite loop.

preconditions: The fix requires guest-triggerable calls to xhci_ring_fetch() or xhci_ring_chain_length(); these occur during normal USB xHCI transfer processing when the guest submits TRBs.
The vulnerability requires the guest to have capability to interact with the emulated xHCI controller — typically requires privileged (root) access in the guest or a compromised guest kernel.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: The vhost-user-gpu device in QEMU (both the virtio-gpu and virgl renderer paths) fails to free iov mappings and buffer allocations across multiple points in the resource lifecycle. Five distinct missing-free sites exist: (1) virgl_cmd_resource_unref drops the attached iov without detach+free, (2) virgl_resource_attach_backing leaks res_iovs on attach_iov failure, (3) vg_resource_create_2d leaks vugbm_buffer in the pixman_image_create_bits error path, (4) vg_resource_destroy does not free res->iov, (5) vg_resource_attach_backing permits double-attach overwriting the old iov pointer.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism:
A guest VM can trigger memory leaks by sending virtio-gpu commands in sequences that leave heap-allocated iov arrays or GPU buffer objects unreachable. Each missing free is reachable via a specific guest-driven command sequence:
- Leak 1 (virgl_cmd_resource_unref): create_resource_2d → attach_backing → resource_unref. The unref path forgets to detach and free the backing iovs before dropping the renderer resource.
- Leak 2 (virgl_resource_attach_backing): attach_backing allocates res_iovs via vg_create_mapping_iov, then passes it to virgl_renderer_resource_attach_iov. If attach_iov returns non-zero, res_iovs is orphaned.
- Leak 3 (vg_resource_create_2d): after vugbm_buffer_create succeeds, if pixman_image_create_bits fails, the error path calls g_free(res) without vugbm_buffer_destroy, leaking the buffer object.
- Leak 4 (vg_resource_destroy): the resource destructor frees res->buffer, res->image, and res itself, but not res->iov. Any backing iovs survive the destroy call.
- Leak 5 (vg_resource_attach_backing): there is no guard checking res->iov before overwriting it. A guest can call attach_backing twice on the same resource; the second call overwrites the pointer without freeing the first allocation.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism:
Each fix commit adds the missing deallocation call or a guard that prevents the leak:
- f6091d86ba9e: In virgl_cmd_resource_unref, adds virgl_renderer_resource_detach_iov() + g_free(res_iovs) before virgl_renderer_resource_unref().
- 63736af5a657: In virgl_resource_attach_backing, captures the return value of virgl_renderer_resource_attach_iov and calls g_free(res_iovs) on failure.
- 86dd8fac2acc: In vg_resource_create_2d error path, adds vugbm_buffer_destroy(&res->buffer) before g_free(res).
- b7afebcf9e6e: In vg_resource_destroy, adds g_free(res->iov) before the other cleanup calls.
- b9f79858a614: In vg_resource_attach_backing, adds a guard 'if (res->iov) { cmd->error = VIRTIO_GPU_RESP_ERR_UNSPEC; return; }' to reject double-attach.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In QEMU NIC emulators, loopback (transmit-to-self) mode calls `nc->info->receive()` directly from the transmit function, bypassing the reentrancy guard (`queue->delivering` flag) that `qemu_net_queue_send()` normally provides. When the receive handler re-enters the transmit path (e.g., through DMA completion, interrupt generation, or packet processing callbacks), this causes unbounded recursion / an infinite loop, consuming CPU or crashing QEMU (CWE-835).
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: The transmit functions of affected NIC emulators (e1000, rtl8139, sungem, lan9118, dp8393x, cadence_gem, pcnet, msf2-emac) detect loopback mode via hardware register flags (e.g., `MII_CR_LOOPBACK`, `PHY_CTRL 0x4000`, `R_CFG1_LB_EN_MASK`, `MAC_XIFCFG_LBCK`, `GEM_NWCTRL_LOCALLOOP`, `pkt->is_loopback`) and deliver the outgoing packet back to the same NIC by calling `nc->info->receive()` (or a device-specific wrapper like `rtl8139_do_receive`, `pcnet_receive`, `lan9118_receive`, `gem_receive`) directly. This direct function-pointer call skips the `queue->delivering` reentrancy check in `qemu_net_queue_send()`. If the receive handler triggers any state change that re-enters the transmit path before the first call returns, a reentrant infinite loop results.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: 1) Infrastructure commit (705df5466c98) introduces `qemu_can_receive_packet()` to check `nc->receive_disabled` and `nc->info->can_receive()` on the target NIC (rather than the sender's peer). It introduces `qemu_receive_packet()` / `qemu_receive_packet_iov()` which route through `qemu_net_queue_receive()` / `qemu_net_queue_receive_iov()`, and these functions check `queue->delivering` and return 0 (dropping the packet) if already in a delivery context, breaking reentrancy. 2) All 10 NIC driver commits replace the direct `nc->info->receive()` call (or device-specific wrapper) in the loopback path with `qemu_receive_packet()`, ensuring the reentrancy check is applied. 3) `qemu_can_send_packet()` is refactored to delegate to `qemu_can_receive_packet()`. 4) `net_tx_pkt.c` uses the iov variant `qemu_receive_packet_iov()` for consistency.

fix_evidence.semantic_change: The fix introduces a new delivery path (qemu_receive_packet -> qemu_net_queue_receive) that is semantically equivalent to qemu_send_packet's path (qemu_send_packet -> qemu_net_queue_send) in terms of reentrancy protection, but designed for the loopback case where there is no sender peer. The key guard is qemu_net_queue_receive's check of queue->delivering: if a packet delivery re-enters the queue while a delivery is already in progress, the reentrant call silently drops the packet, breaking the infinite recursion. Additionally, the new path checks can_receive/receive_disabled on the target NIC itself (via qemu_can_receive_packet) rather than on the sender's peer, which is correct for loopback where sender and receiver are the same NIC.

configuration_guards: Loopback mode is gated by NIC-specific hardware register bits (e.g., MII_CR_LOOPBACK, R_CFG1_LB_EN_MASK, MAC_XIFCFG_LBCK, phy_control 0x4000, GEM_NWCTRL_LOCALLOOP, is_loopback flag, SONIC_TCR). The vulnerability is only reachable when these loopback bits are set.
pkt->is_loopback flag in net_tx_pkt.c is set by the virtual device layer when the NIC operates in loopback mode.
version_or_feature_guards: Vulnerability exists in all QEMU versions up to and including 5.2.0 that include the affected NIC emulators. The fix was applied in QEMU 6.0.0-rc1 and backported to stable branches.
preconditions: Guest OS or guest userspace must be able to set the NIC's loopback mode (typically via PHY register writes or MAC configuration register writes).
Guest must trigger packet transmission while loopback is active.

The NIC's receive handler must trigger a side effect that re-enters the transmit path (e.g., generating an interrupt that causes the guest to write new transmit descriptors, which the emulator processes before the first transmit returns).

For the rtl8139 case specifically, the original call passed do_interrupt (interrupt enable flag), and the replacement drops interrupts entirely for loopback mode (qemu_receive_packet does not generate interrupts).

The fix extends this guard to qemu_net_queue_receive().

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: The BACapp dissector function fAbstractSyntaxNType performs recursive descent parsing of BACnet abstract syntax types with no recursion depth limit. The function calls sub-dissectors (fActionList, fContextTaggedValue, fApplicationTypes, fReadAccessSpecification, fDeviceObjectReference, etc.) which in turn call back to fAbstractSyntaxNType at 15+ call sites. A specially crafted BACnet packet with deeply nested abstract syntax types causes unbounded recursion leading to stack exhaustion and crash.
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: CWE-674 (Uncontrolled Recursion). fAbstractSyntaxNType at packet-bacapp.c:7972 (parent commit 8511253a) has zero recursion tracking. Its while-loop dispatches to sub-dissectors that call fAbstractSyntaxNType back. The only loop guard 'if (offset <= lastoffset) break;' prevents infinite loops on the same offset but does not bound nesting depth. An attacker crafts deeply nested BACnet opening/closing tags to overflow the C stack.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Add per-packet recursion depth tracking via proto_data API. dissect_bacapp initializes depth=0. fAbstractSyntaxNType increments depth on entry, checks against BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH=100, emits expert warning and returns early if exceeded, decrements on exit. Also registers expert field ei_bacapp_max_recursion_depth_reached and adds #include <epan/proto_data.h>.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: Three unbounded `for (;;)` loops in `dissect_rfc7468` (epan/dissectors/file-rfc7468.c) call `tvb_find_line_end(tvb, offset, -1, &next_offset, FALSE)` with reassembly=FALSE. When offset is past the tvbuff end, tvb_find_line_end returns a zero-length line without advancing next_offset, so next_offset == offset and the loop never makes progress, causing infinite spin and denial of service (CWE-835).
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: The function `dissect_rfc7468` contains three `for (;;)` loops at lines 153, 215, and 260 of the vulnerable parent commit (b9e299b1a38). Each loop calls `tvb_find_line_end(tvb, offset, -1, &next_offset, FALSE)`. Per the commit message from fix commit b3215d99cacb: when past the end of the tvbuff with reassembly=FALSE, tvb_find_line_end returns a zero-length line and does NOT advance next_offset. The `for (;;)` construct has no bounds check, so the loop spins forever (CPU exhaustion / DoS). An attacker can trigger this via a crafted capture file or packet injection that causes the dissector to read past the tvbuff boundary. No removed critical sequence exists; the vulnerability is purely a missing input-validation guard (tvuff bounds check).
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Replace all three `for (;;)` loop headers with `while (tvb_offset_exists(tvb, offset))`. This adds a tvbuff bounds check as the loop continuation condition: before each iteration, verify that offset is still within valid tvbuff data. If offset has advanced past the end, the loop terminates immediately. All loop body logic (line parsing, boundary detection, base64 decoding) is preserved unchanged.

source_refs.snippet: rfc7468: don't use for (;;) for tvb_find_line_end() loops. If you're past the end of the tvbuff, tvb_find_line_end() will, when reassembly is not being done, return a zero-length line without advancing next_offset, so, unless you check for being past the end of the tvbuff, you'll loop forever. Fixes #17801.

reviewed_vet.root_cause_summary: In dissect_gquic_tag(), the default: case for unknown/undecoded GQUIC tag types does tag_offset += tag_len and continues the while(tag_number) loop without validating tag_offset progress or total_tag_len overflow. An attacker can craft a packet where each unknown tag's offset_end equals the current tag_offset, making tag_len=0 and tag_offset never advance, causing the while loop to iterate up to tag_number times (bounded by a 16-bit field via the caller, ~65535 per call, with additional iterations possible via nested SCFG calls) without meaningful data consumption — a CPU-exhaustion infinite loop (CWE-835).
reviewed_vet.vulnerability_mechanism: The while(tag_number) loop in dissect_gquic_tag() reads a 4-byte tag type and 4-byte offset_end from the packet each iteration, computes tag_len = offset_end - tag_offset where tag_offset starts at 0 and accumulates per tag. In the default: switch case for unrecognized tags, the vulnerable code does tag_offset += tag_len and breaks. When offset_end == tag_offset for a given tag, tag_len=0 and tag_offset does not advance. The post-switch check (if tag_offset != offset_end -> corrects tag_offset = offset_end) is satisfied, and tag_number-- continues the loop. Since the next iteration reads a fresh offset_end from attacker-controlled packet data at the advanced offset pointer, the attacker can supply offset_end values that perpetually match tag_offset, producing endless zero-length tags. tag_number is derived from a 16-bit field (max ~65535 per call), and nested calls from TAG_SCFG processing can compound the total iteration count, causing sustained CPU consumption (infinite loop, CWE-835). No overflow guard on total_tag_len exists (CWE-682) — the return expression offset + total_tag_len can wrap around if tag_len values accumulate beyond UINT32_MAX, though the infinite loop is the primary exploitation path.
reviewed_vet.fix_mechanism: Three coordinated changes in epan/dissectors/packet-gquic.c: (1) In the default: case, replace tag_offset += tag_len with goto end; to immediately exit the function for unknown tags instead of continuing the loop. This eliminates the infinite loop path. (2) Add an end: label after the loop with an overflow guard: if (offset + total_tag_len <= offset) detects integer wraparound (or total_tag_len=0), and returns offset + tvb_reported_length_remaining(tvb, offset) to safely skip remaining malformed tag data. (3) Register a new expert field ei_gquic_length_invalid in proto_register_gquic() with its static declaration, to report the invalid-length condition to the user.

source_refs.snippet: added end: label with overflow guard: if (offset + total_tag_len <= offset) { expert_add_info_format(... "Invalid total tag length: %u", total_tag_len); return offset + tvb_reported_length_remaining(tvb, offset); }
added ei_gquic_length_invalid registration and trailing comma to ei_gquic_invalid_parameter
GQUIC: make sure our tag offset advances. Make sure gquic tag offset advances so that we don't infinitely loop.

necessary_conditions: A crafted GQUIC packet reaches dissect_gquic_tag() with at least one tag whose type triggers the default: case (unrecognized/undecoded tag)
The attacker controls the 4-byte offset_end fields in the packet such that offset_end == tag_offset for one or more tags, producing tag_len=0
tag_number is non-zero (16-bit field supplied by caller, up to 65535 per call; additional nesting via TAG_SCFG can compound iterations)
vulnerable_sequences: dissect_gquic_tag: while(tag_number) loop iteration → read 4-byte tag type + 4-byte offset_end from packet at offset → tag_len = offset_end - tag_offset → switch default: case → tag_offset += tag_len (no-op when tag_len=0) → break → post-switch tag_offset == offset_end (satisfied) → tag_number-- → loop repeats with fresh offset_end from attacker-controlled packet data
missing_guards: No progress invariant on tag_offset — the loop has no check that tag_offset actually increases between iterations
No upper bound on total tag data processed relative to remaining packet length
No early exit from the while loop when processing an unknown/undecoded tag type
No integer overflow guard on total_tag_len before the return expression offset + total_tag_len

fix_guards: goto end; in default: case — immediately exits the function for unknown tags, preventing any further loop iterations
if (offset + total_tag_len <= offset) — integer overflow detection on total_tag_len accumulation; catches both total_tag_len=0 and wraparound cases
return offset + tvb_reported_length_remaining(tvb, offset) — safely skips remaining malformed tag region when overflow is detected
changed_sequences: default: { ... tag_offset += tag_len; break; } → default: { ... goto end; break; }
Added end: label after while loop with overflow check before final return offset + total_tag_len
Added static expert_field ei_gquic_length_invalid = EI_INIT; declaration
Added ei_gquic_length_invalid registration entry and trailing comma in proto_register_gquic() expert array
fix_evidence.semantic_change: Unknown/undecoded tag processing changes from 'advance through and continue parsing more tags' to 'immediately exit the function'. This is semantically correct because GQUIC tags are TLV-like entries where an unrecognized tag type means the dissector cannot interpret the boundary of the tag's value; continuing to iterate invites offset desynchronization and unbounded loops. The overflow guard provides a safety net for the normal return path.

preconditions: The GQUIC dissector must be enabled (default in Wireshark builds with GQUIC support)
The crafted packet must reach the dissect_gquic_tag code path, which handles GQUIC tags within server config, server update, or similar GQUIC handshake messages
